title: Economic Policy Review: Recent Innovations in Treasury Cash Management

source.name: Federal Reserve Bank of New York

summary: This 2004 Federal Reserve Bank of New York Current Issues in Economics and Finance research paper examines three innovations in the Treasury Tax and Loan Program: electronic tax collection via EFTPS, Dynamic Investment, and the Term Investment Option. The authors find these changes improved cost efficiency in processing tax payments, enhanced reserve stabilization in the banking system, and shifted a portion of Treasury deposit interest rate determination to market processes.
key_points: Electronic Federal Tax Payment System (EFTPS) reduced processing costs for business tax payments and improved return on public funds by reducing overnight balances in interest-free accounts
Dynamic Investment acts as an automatic stabilizer, keeping Treasury's Reserve Bank balance closer to target level
The Term Investment Option shifted interest rate determination for some Treasury deposits to a competitive bidding process
The Term Investment Option generated, on average, an additional 17 basis points of yield on Treasury deposit balances
Authors are Kenneth D. Garbade (VP, Capital Markets Function), John C. Partlan (Markets Officer), and Paul J. Santoro (Senior Financial Analyst)
why_it_matters: This paper documents structural changes to Treasury cash management operations and the federal tax collection process implemented in the early 2000s. Understanding these historical innovations provides context for how Treasury market plumbing evolved pre-financial crisis. This 2004 publication represents historical market infrastructure documentation but has limited current relevance for market participants.
market_structure_context: The paper addresses market structure topics including: Treasury cash management operations via the Treasury Tax and Loan Program, Reserve Bank balance management, and the operational mechanics of government cash management. The three innovations examined (EFTPS, Dynamic Investment, Term Investment Option) represent infrastructure changes to how Treasury interacted with the banking system and managed federal tax receipts in the 2004-era framework.
important_numbers: 17 basis points (additional yield from Term Investment Option)
